items.title: Add 'No hidden fees' statement near rental form CTA
items.description: Competitors (Triangle Lawn Games, Reventals) explicitly advertise 'no extra fees' as a selling point because customers are often burned by surprise charges. Adding a single line 'No hidden fees — total shown before you confirm' near the form submit button directly addresses the #1 reason customers abandon rental bookings.
items.source: Competitor analysis: trianglelawngames.com, reventals.com; cart abandonment research: foursixty.com/blog

items.title: Show live total price in rental form as dates are selected
items.description: When a customer picks rental dates in the form, the total price (pricePerDay × days) should update in real time and be prominently displayed before the submit button. Research shows dynamic price transparency is the single biggest checkout conversion lever for rental services.
items.source: UX research: unicornplatform.com/blog/car-rental-booking-pages-in-2026; Baymard checkout optimization

items.title: Category filter chips overflow on mobile without horizontal scroll
items.description: On 375px screens the 6 category pills wrap onto two rows or overflow, breaking the layout. The filter bar needs a horizontally scrollable single row with touch scrolling on mobile — common pattern used by Airbnb, Etsy, and all major marketplace apps.
items.source: PRODUCT.md mobile-first principle; Smashing Magazine booking UX case study

items.title: All game images need descriptive alt text
items.description: Screen reader users receive no information about game appearance since images have empty or generic alt attributes. Each game card image should have an alt like 'Giant Jenga — oversized wooden block tower stacked outdoors'. Required for WCAG 2.1 AA compliance.
items.source: WCAG 2.1 AA audit guidance: boia.org/blog/web-accessibility-for-travel-agencies; accessibe.com accessible rental properties

items.title: Category filter and player chip groups need keyboard navigation
items.description: Filter chip groups should follow the ARIA radio group pattern so keyboard users can Tab to the group and use arrow keys to change selection. Currently a keyboard-only user must Tab through every individual button. Required for WCAG 2.1 AA.
items.source: WCAG 2.1 accessible booking flow guidelines: frontdesksuite.com/en/product/accessibility

items.title: Show cancellation policy on game modal before rental form
items.description: Customers want to know cancellation terms before committing to fill out a form. Competitors like bookextraordinary.com advertise 'free cancellation up to 24 hours' prominently. Adding a short cancellation policy line in the GameModal builds confidence at the decision stage.
items.source: Competitor analysis: bookextraordinary.com; rental UX: techspokes.com/blog/vacation-rental-website-conversions

items.title: Event package bundles (e.g. 'Party Pack: 3 games for $X/day')
items.description: Multiple competitor sites (bookextraordinary.com, reventals.com) offer curated event packages bundling 3-4 complementary games at a slight discount. This increases average order value and helps indecisive customers. Could be implemented as a featured section above the game grid with hardcoded bundles.
items.source: Competitor analysis: bookextraordinary.com, reventals.com/blog/lawn-game-rental-ideas
items.pmVerdict: rejected: bundle pricing requires multi-game discount logic outside the per-day pricing model; out of current product scope and risks scope creep into the discount/promo system explicitly excluded in PRODUCT.md

items.title: Inline form field validation (not just on submit)
items.description: Research from Baymard shows that forms validating only on submit cause 22% more errors and take longer to complete. Each field in the rental form should validate on blur — name required, email format, phone format — with inline green/red feedback so customers correct errors as they go.
items.source: Baymard Institute checkout UX; alfdesigngroup.com/post/checkout-ux; quape.com/checkout-ux-cart-abandonment

items.title: Game modal 'What's included' section (setup, pickup/delivery, accessories)
items.description: Customers frequently ask what exactly they're getting with a rental — is setup included? What accessories come with the game? Adding a short bullet list 'Included: boards, 8 bean bags, carrying bag — delivery available' inside the GameModal removes a key barrier to booking.
items.source: Customer pain point: vendor communication frustration (reventals.com customer research); competitor UX patterns

items.title: Rental form has no inline date validation (past dates accepted)
items.description: The current rental form date inputs allow selecting past dates or end dates before start dates without any feedback. This creates confusing behaviour and erroneous orders. Date fields should validate: start ≥ today, end ≥ start + 1, with inline error messages.
items.source: Equipment rental UX: eliftech.com/insights/how-to-improve-booking-experience; hotel booking engine problems: traveltekpro.com

items.title: Add 'Most Popular' badge to top games
items.description: Social proof badges ('Most Popular', 'Fan Favourite', 'Perfect for weddings') on 2-3 game cards reduce decision paralysis. Gigantogames.com and competitors use these prominently. Can be implemented as a hardcoded badge field on the Game type.
items.source: Competitor analysis: gigantogames.com; trust signal research: mobiloud.com/blog/trust-signals-cart-abandonment

items.title: Game modal backdrop click accidentally dismisses the modal
items.description: Clicking the dark overlay around the game modal closes it. Users reading game details or filling dates tap the edges of the modal and lose their context. Industry standard is to require explicit close button click or Escape key to dismiss modals with form state.
items.source: UX best practice; e-commerce modal patterns: uxpin.com/studio/blog/web-layout-best-practices
items.pmVerdict: rejected: backlog is healthy at 25+ pending stories; this is a medium-confidence UX preference (some users expect backdrop-to-close) rather than a clear usability defect — defer to a future run when priority stories are cleared

items.title: Quick 'days' selector on game card showing instant total price
items.description: Add a simple '1 day / 2 days / Weekend' toggle directly on each game card that instantly shows the total price without opening the modal. Car rental sites (Hertz, Enterprise) show this as their primary conversion mechanic — customers convert 30% faster when they see total price before clicking.
items.source: Car rental booking UX: unicornplatform.com/blog/car-rental-booking-pages-in-2026; e-commerce UX: digitalapplied.com/blog/ecommerce-checkout-optimization-2026
items.pmVerdict: rejected: medium confidence + medium effort; US-021 already provides live price in the rental form; adding a redundant price widget on the card risks cluttering the grid UI; defer until post-US-021 user testing reveals a gap

items.title: Skeleton loading state for game grid on initial page load
items.description: The game grid renders all at once after JS hydration, causing a flash of unstyled content on slow connections. Adding a CSS skeleton loader (pulsing grey card outlines) prevents layout shift and makes the page felt faster. Required by PRODUCT.md design principle 5.
items.source: PRODUCT.md design principle 5: 'no layout shift on load'; core web vitals CLS best practices

items.title: Add service area / delivery radius notice in the hero section
items.description: Customers landing on the site don't know if the service covers their area. Adding 'Serving [City/Region] and surrounding areas' in the hero prevents customers from spending time building a booking only to discover at checkout there is no delivery to their location.
items.source: Competitor pattern: all competitor sites prominently show service city (dallaslawngames.com, sanantoniolawngames.com); customer frustration research
items.pmVerdict: accepted: static informational text in the hero, not delivery tracking or logistics; addresses a key early-funnel conversion barrier; feasible as a shared constant in Next.js

items.title: Floating 'Book Now' sticky CTA on mobile product browsing
items.description: On mobile, the 'Rent Now' button inside GameModal is only visible after scrolling down. A sticky bottom bar with 'Rent [Game Name] — $X/day — Book Now' pinned to the bottom of the modal on mobile keeps the conversion action always visible without requiring scroll.
items.source: Mobile conversion optimization: parallelhq.com/blog/ux-design-to-improve-website-conversions; mobile-first booking UX: goodshuffle.com/blog/event-rental-website-builder
items.pmVerdict: rejected: run cap of 5 stories reached; medium confidence — sticky bars on modals can cause z-index conflicts and layout issues; defer to next PM run after existing modal stories (US-025, US-027) are dev-complete so the interaction can be assessed together

items.title: Rental form inputs missing visible focus rings and ARIA error IDs
items.description: Form inputs lose their focus ring in some browsers (removed via outline:none), making keyboard navigation invisible. Each input also needs aria-describedby pointing to its error message element for screen reader users. Required for WCAG 2.1 AA compliance.
items.source: WCAG 2.1 form accessibility guidelines; accessible booking: simplyscheduleappointments.com/accessibility-booking-plugin
items.pmVerdict: rejected: duplicate of US-030 (Rental form inputs: visible focus rings and ARIA error associations) which already covers focus ring restoration and aria-describedby linkage for all form inputs

items.title: 'Games for events' quick-filter links on homepage (Wedding, Corporate, Kids Party)
items.description: Competitors use occasion-based navigation ('Wedding games', 'Corporate events', 'Kids birthday') as a primary discovery path. Adding 3 occasion-based quick-filter buttons above the game grid lets customers find relevant games by use case instead of scrolling through all categories.
items.source: Competitor UX: bookextraordinary.com, rusticvibegames.com; event planning trends: tentandtable.net/blog/post/trends-in-party-rentals
items.pmVerdict: accepted: occasion-based discovery is a genuine new capability not covered by existing category/player/search filters; feasible as client-side tag filtering with a new optional field on Game type; addresses real competitor gap

items.title: Empty state in game grid should include a CTA to contact or browse all
items.description: When filters return zero results, the 'No games found' message is a dead end — no link back to all games, no call to action. Per PRODUCT.md design principle 3 ('never a dead end'), the zero-results state needs a 'Browse all games' button and optionally a 'Request a custom game' link.
items.source: PRODUCT.md design principle; e-commerce empty state UX best practices

items.title: Add game dimensions and weight to the modal so customers know what to expect
items.description: Customers booking for events need to know if a game fits their space. The games.ts already has a dimensions field but GameModal doesn't visually highlight it. A prominent 'Setup space needed: 10ft × 10ft' callout with a simple icon would directly answer a key pre-booking question.
items.source: Customer pain point research: uncertainty about physical setup requirements; competitor analysis showing setup details prominently
items.pmVerdict: rejected: duplicate of US-032 (Setup space callout in game modal) which already covers a prominent dimensions callout with icon inside GameModal reusing the existing dimensions field

items.title: Testimonials section has no link to more reviews or review source
items.description: The testimonials section shows 3 hardcoded reviews with no source attribution (no Google, Yelp, or similar), making them look fake to skeptical customers. At minimum, adding a star rating and a city name to each testimonial increases perceived authenticity.
items.source: Trust signal research: mobiloud.com/blog/trust-signals-cart-abandonment; B2C trust patterns for service businesses
items.pmVerdict: accepted: PRODUCT.md explicitly states 'trust signals matter'; small effort change to static data that increases social proof authenticity; no external services or analytics required

items.title: Micro-event 'Backyard Party' bundle suggestion based on cart contents
items.description: The 2025 micro-events trend (smaller backyard gatherings) means customers often want 2-3 complementary games. After a customer adds a game to the cart or opens a modal, show 'Complete your backyard party: customers who rented [Giant Jenga] also rented [Cornhole Set]'. Implemented purely in-memory, no backend needed.
items.source: Event planning trends 2025-2026: tentandtable.net/blog/post/trends-in-party-rentals, accio.com/business/event-rental-trends; e-commerce cross-sell patterns
items.pmVerdict: rejected: medium confidence + medium effort; backlog is healthy at 27 pending stories; cross-sell recommendation logic adds complexity to cart/modal interaction that should be deferred until the cart (US-008) is dev-complete and user-tested; risk of clutter in modal alongside US-025 and US-027

items.title: Game modal has no scroll affordance on mobile — rental form below the fold is missed
items.description: On 375px screens the GameModal content (description, dimensions, included items, cancellation policy, rental form) is taller than the viewport, but there is no bottom-fade gradient or scroll cue to signal more content below. Customers may never see the rental form. A fade-out shadow at the modal's bottom edge is the standard pattern to signal scrollability.
items.source: Mobile UX pattern research: ralabs.org/blog/booking-ux-best-practices; peer-to-peer rental platform patterns (shipturtle.com/blog/peer-to-peer-rental-marketplace)
items.pmVerdict: accepted: high confidence ux-bug directly blocking customers from seeing the rental form on mobile; small effort CSS-only change; no existing story covers this; addresses PRODUCT.md mobile-first principle

items.title: Display minimum rental period label on each game card
items.description: Competitors like Upstate Jamboree and Triangle Lawn Games show minimum rental periods (e.g. '1-day minimum') directly on game listings. Customers wanting a half-day rental will abandon before reaching checkout if this is not set upfront. A short 'Min. 1 day' line on the GameCard prevents wasted form completions and sets accurate expectations.
items.source: Competitor analysis: upstatejamboree.com/lawn-game-rentals, trianglelawngames.com; Baymard checkout abandonment research: baymard.com/lists/cart-abandonment-rate

items.title: FAQ accordion section on the homepage answering top pre-booking questions
items.description: Rental service customers consistently ask the same questions before booking: what's included, how delivery works, what if it rains, what's the cancellation policy. A collapsible FAQ section (5–7 questions) near the bottom of the homepage reduces support contact and addresses booking objections at the browsing stage, increasing overall conversion.
items.source: Competitor pattern: bookextraordinary.com FAQ section; goodshuffle.com event rental UX; Baymard trust signal research showing policy transparency reduces abandonment (baymard.com/blog/cart-abandonment)

items.title: Add a social proof counter ('500+ events served') to the hero section
items.description: Event rental competitors (Wonderfly Games, bookextraordinary.com) show cumulative social proof numbers to reassure first-time visitors. A hardcoded or localStorage-derived counter in the hero section converts skeptical visitors faster by demonstrating the service is established. A simple stat like '500+ happy customers' or '200+ events served' is low effort with measurable trust impact.
items.source: Competitor analysis: wonderflygames.com, bookextraordinary.com; trust signal research: mobiloud.com/blog/trust-signals-cart-abandonment

items.title: Navbar has no active page indicator as routes expand
items.description: With routes like /profile, /admin, and /order-confirmation being added, the Navbar currently has no visual treatment distinguishing the active page. Users lose orientation context, especially on mobile. Active nav links should have a distinct underline or colour treatment. A standard Next.js usePathname hook approach keeps this to under 10 lines of code.
items.source: UX navigation convention: Nielsen Norman Group; e-commerce checkout UX: digitalapplied.com/blog/ecommerce-checkout-optimization-2026

items.title: Game modal is missing focus trap — keyboard users Tab out into background page content
items.description: When GameModal is open, pressing Tab eventually moves focus out of the modal into the hidden page behind it. WCAG 2.1 SC 2.1.2 (No Keyboard Trap in the positive sense — focus must stay inside the dialog) and the ARIA modal dialog pattern both require focus to cycle within the open modal. Focus must also return to the trigger element on close.
items.source: WCAG 2.1 SC 2.1.2; ARIA dialog modal pattern: w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/dialog-modal; webability.io/compliance/wcag/wcag-2025-guide

items.title: Game modal missing role='dialog', aria-modal, and aria-labelledby — screen readers get no dialog context
items.description: Without role='dialog', aria-modal='true', and aria-labelledby pointing to the modal title, NVDA and VoiceOver users hear no indication they've entered a modal context and cannot use dialog-specific navigation shortcuts. This is a straightforward ARIA annotation fix that unblocks screen reader users from using the entire booking flow.
items.source: ARIA dialog pattern: w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/patterns/dialog-modal; WCAG 2.1 AA; accessibility audit guidance: webability.io/compliance/wcag/wcag-2025-guide

items.title: Add a step progress indicator to the planned multi-step checkout wizard
items.description: Baymard research shows checkout abandonment drops when customers can see remaining steps. The planned 3-step rental wizard (US-006) should include a visible progress bar or step label ('Step 2 of 3 — Contact Info') at the top so customers know they are nearly done. Removing completion uncertainty is one of the highest-ROI checkout optimisations.
items.source: Baymard Institute checkout UX: baymard.com/lists/cart-abandonment-rate; booking UX best practices 2025: ralabs.org/blog/booking-ux-best-practices; ulansoftware.com/blog/ux-tips-improve-travel-booking-conversion

items.title: Weather disclaimer on outdoor game cards nudging customers to ask about rain contingency
items.description: Event planners booking outdoor games (Cornhole, Giant Jenga, Bocce Ball) frequently worry about rain contingency — this concern surfaced consistently in wedding planning communities. A small informational tag 'Outdoor game — ask about indoor setup options' on applicable game cards addresses this objection at the decision stage and reduces last-minute cancellations.
items.source: WeddingWire community discussions: weddingwire.com/wedding-forums/lawn-games-yay-or-nay; The Knot reddit wedding planning: theknot.com/content/reddit-wedding-planning; competitor analysis

items.title: Confirm Next.js Image component is used for all game images for WebP optimization
items.description: If any game images use a standard HTML tag rather than Next.js Image (next/image), they miss automatic WebP conversion, responsive srcSet generation, and lazy loading. On a grid of 8 full-width images this measurably hurts LCP on mobile. All game images should use next/image with appropriate width, height, and priority props on above-the-fold cards.
items.source: Next.js Image component docs: nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/components/image; Core Web Vitals LCP optimisation for image-heavy grids; Google PageSpeed Insights guidance 2025

items.title: Rental form submit button has no loading/disabled state — double-submits create duplicate orders
items.description: After tapping 'Submit' on the rental form, if the button has no disabled or loading state, customers who tap it twice (or on a slow device) will create duplicate order entries in localStorage. Adding a spinner and disabling the button on first click prevents duplicate orders and gives clear feedback that the request is being processed.
items.source: Form UX best practices: Baymard Institute; checkout UX: alfdesigngroup.com/post/checkout-ux; booking flow UX: paylode.com/use-cases/booking-flows

items.title: Recently viewed games horizontal strip persisted in localStorage
items.description: Customers who browse multiple games and later want to return to one they liked must re-scroll the entire grid. A 'Recently Viewed' horizontal strip (max 4 games) below the main grid, persisted in localStorage, reduces back-and-forth navigation. Zero backend required — add/retrieve a rg_recent_views key following existing localStorage key conventions.
items.source: E-commerce UX: Airbnb, Viator recently-viewed patterns; peer-to-peer rental platform UX: shipturtle.com/blog/peer-to-peer-rental-marketplace-platform

items.title: Add 'Skip to main content' link at top of page for keyboard and screen reader users
items.description: Keyboard and screen reader users must Tab through all navbar links before reaching the game grid on every page. A visually-hidden 'Skip to main content' anchor link as the very first element in the layout is WCAG 2.1 AA required (SC 2.4.1 Bypass Blocks) and takes approximately 5 lines of Tailwind CSS to implement correctly.
items.source: WCAG 2.1 SC 2.4.1 Bypass Blocks; web accessibility guide 2026: webability.io/blog/wcag-ada-2025-making-websites-accessible-for-all; inclusiveweb.co/understanding-wcag

items.title: Admin settings page for live-editing service area text and cancellation policy copy
items.description: The service area notice (US-031) and cancellation policy (US-025) are hardcoded constants. A simple /admin/settings page with two editable text fields — persisted to localStorage using rg_settings — lets the business owner update key customer-facing copy without a code deploy. This is the highest-value admin self-service capability after inventory management.
items.source: Event rental software trends 2026: gomomentus.com/blog/revolutionizing-event-and-venue-management-event-trends; party rental platform admin self-service patterns

items.title: Add 'Instant Confirmation' trust badge near the rental form submit button
items.description: A key anxiety for first-time rental customers is 'will someone actually confirm this or does it sit in a queue for days?' Adding an 'Instant Confirmation' badge or a one-line note ('Your booking is confirmed immediately') near the form CTA directly addresses this fear. Airbnb and Viator use this pattern prominently — it removes one of the last reasons to hesitate before submitting.
items.source: Airbnb Experiences and Viator 'Instant Confirmation' trust badge patterns; rental trust signals: accessibe.com/blog/knowledgebase/accessible-short-term-rental-property; conversion optimisation: spiltmilkwebdesign.com/conversion-rate-optimization-website-ux-for-boutique-hotels-in-2025

items.title: Add 'Backyard BBQ' and 'Beach Day' as occasion filter options to capture micro-event demand
items.description: 2025–2026 party rental trend data shows strong growth in informal micro-events and outdoor gatherings beyond weddings and corporate events. Adding 'Backyard BBQ' and 'Beach Day' occasion tags (extending the occasions system from US-033) captures this growing customer segment and differentiates from competitors still primarily targeting formal events.
items.source: Party rental trends 2025: tentandtable.net/blog/post/trends-in-party-rentals; event rental trends: accio.com/business/event-rental-trends; event industry 2026: whova.com/blog/top-event-industry-trends-2025-incorporate-events

items.title: Floating 'Back to top' button on mobile to return to filters without full scroll
items.description: On mobile, after a customer browses the game grid they must scroll all the way back to the top to change category or search filters. A floating 'Back to top' button (visible after scrolling 300px, positioned bottom-right above any sticky elements) reduces the effort of re-filtering and keeps customers engaged rather than bouncing.
items.source: Mobile UX patterns: medium.com/@myousufraza/car-rental-mobile-app-ui-ux-design-in-2025; mobile booking conversion: ulansoftware.com/blog/ux-tips-improve-travel-booking-conversion

items.title: Game comparison panel: select 2–3 games to compare price, players, and dimensions side-by-side
items.description: Customers booking for parties often compare 2–3 similar games (e.g. Giant Jenga vs Giant Connect Four) before deciding. A lightweight comparison panel — triggered by a 'Compare' checkbox on game cards, showing a side-by-side table of price, players, dimensions, and included items — helps indecisive customers commit without bouncing to a competitor.
items.source: E-commerce comparison UX patterns; rental marketplace UX: easternpeak.com/blog/rental-marketplace-development-airbnb-example; peer-to-peer rental: shipturtle.com/blog/peer-to-peer-rental-marketplace

items.title: No toast or success feedback after wishlist toggle — users don't know if the action worked
items.description: When the planned wishlist feature (US-013) is built, toggling a heart on a game card must provide immediate feedback. Without a toast notification ('Added to wishlist' / 'Removed from wishlist'), especially on mobile where heart icons are small, users tap the button repeatedly unsure if the action registered. A 2-second toast is the industry standard for silent-state toggles.
items.source: Mobile UX feedback patterns: Airbnb, Viator wishlist interactions; e-commerce micro-interaction best practices: mobiloud.com/blog/trust-signals-cart-abandonment

items.title: Game detail page at /games/[id] as an alternative to modal-only flow
items.description: The current GameModal approach means games have no shareable URL — customers can't send a friend a link to a specific game or bookmark it. Creating a /games/[id] static page for each game (using Next.js dynamic routes) enables sharing, improves SEO for the game names, and gives an alternative non-modal flow on mobile where modals can be finicky.
items.source: Rental platform UX: easternpeak.com/blog/rental-marketplace-development-airbnb-example; peer-to-peer rental SEO patterns (shipturtle.com); vacation rental booking engines: martalebre.com/blog/best-vacation-rental-booking-engines

items.title: Add a compact 'How it works' step-summary above the game grid to orient first-time visitors
items.description: The existing HowItWorks section is at the bottom of the page — far below where most browsing decisions are made. Many first-time visitors start interacting with game cards without understanding the rental process. A compact 3-icon strip ('Browse → Book Online → Delivered to You') above the game grid orients customers before they engage with the catalog and sets positive expectations.
items.source: Competitor UX: trianglelawngames.com and wonderflygames.com both show process steps near the top; conversion UX: spiltmilkwebdesign.com/conversion-rate-optimization-website-ux-for-boutique-hotels-in-2025
